name: mediterranean-villa
property: Modern minimalist villa with infinity pool overlooking the Mediterranean Sea, white concrete and glass facade, Santorini-inspired palette
prompt: Hasselblad X2D 100C with XCD 21mm f/4 lens — wide-angle architectural sweep; f/8, ISO 100, 1/60s, tripod-mounted, circular polarizer, RAW capture. Golden hour / magic hour daylight: sun low (~18° above horizon) positioned to the left-rear (west-southwest) casting long warm directional light across the scene; warm amber highlights on the white concrete facade, glass rim-lighting and crisp reflections in the infinity pool, soft elongated shadows emphasizing texture in plaster and honed travertine. Supplementary lighting: subtle hidden warm uplighting along lower facade and steps, thin linear pool LED lights gently glowing, warm interior glow visible through floor-to-ceiling windows. Sky: clear with wispy cirrus clouds for visual drama, slight golden haze at the horizon. Camera position and composition: low vantage ~1.2 m (4 ft) above decking, ~6 m back from pool edge, slight left-of-center so the villa sits on the right third of the frame and the infinity pool edge leads the eye toward the Mediterranean horizon on the left third — strong leading lines from the pool edge and decking planks, layered depth with foreground olives and bougainvillea, mid-ground pool and sun loungers, mid/near villa, and background sea and sky. Framing devices: overhanging pergola beam at upper-left and a sculptural olive tree at lower-left to frame and add scale. Mood & color grading: Santorini-inspired palette — pristine white concrete, sea-blue reflections, warm golden highlights and cool blue shadows, rich earth tones in wood and travertine; slight warm haze over the distant islands. Micro-details: razor-sharp glass clarity with accurate reflections, visible grain in white concrete, soft veining in stone tiles, sparkling sun glints on slight pool ripples, creamy buttery bokeh on distant islands and foliage. Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality (Architectural Digest/Wallpaper*), aspirational yet believable lived-in luxury, no people in frame, tasteful outdoor furniture (minimal olive-wood chaise lounges, linen umbrellas) and mature landscaping to enhance composition.

name: malibu-mansion
property: Beachfront luxury mansion in Malibu with floor-to-ceiling windows, Pacific Ocean view, contemporary California architecture
prompt: Sony A7R V with Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II (set to ~18mm) — wide-angle sweep; aperture f/8, ISO 100, shutter 1/60s, tripod-mounted, RAW capture for maximum dynamic range. Golden hour / magic hour daylight: sun low on the western horizon, positioned slightly camera-left and behind the house so warm directional light creates a rim-lit silhouette on the roofline and long, soft shadows stretching toward the camera. Light interaction: warm sunlight glancing across warm-stained cedar cladding and honed limestone terraces, bright highlights and crisp specular reflections on floor-to-ceiling glass, gentle sparkle on the Pacific and mirror-like infinity pool edge; hidden warm uplighting along the facade and recessed soffit lights softly illuminate overhangs, interior rooms emit a subtle warm glow through windows to suggest lived-in luxury. Sky: clear with a few high wispy cirrus clouds for drama, faint golden haze near horizon. Camera position and composition: low, slightly left-of-center vantage at ~1.2 m (4 ft) height, anchored on the beachfront deck so the curved infinity pool edge and teak boardwalk form leading lines from lower-left foreground into the mid-ground architecture and then toward the ocean; place the main two-story glass pavilion on the right third of the frame (rule of thirds), allowing expansive seascape and sky on the left third for breathability. Framing devices: native dune grasses and sculpted olive planters in the immediate foreground (soft bokeh), a frameless glass balustrade and architectural overhangs create layered depth. Depth layers: textured sand and landscaping foreground, pool/terrace and glass façade mid-ground, Pacific horizon and sky background. Mood and grading: warm golden highlights, cool blue-violet shadows, rich earth tones in wood and stone, slight golden haze for atmosphere; photorealistic, ultra-detailed micro-textures — visible wood grain, stone veining, crisp glass reflections, subtle water ripples. Bokeh: creamy on foreground grasses, clean transition into sharp architecture. Magazine-quality, aspirational but real, no people, include tasteful outdoor furniture (two chaise lounges, folded towel) and a sculptural potted olive to suggest living without distracting. Not a render — hyper-real photographic capture suitable for Architectural Digest cover.

name: bali-resort-villa
property: Tropical Balinese-style resort villa surrounded by rice terraces, teak wood and volcanic stone, lush vegetation
prompt: Camera: Sony A7R V; Lens: Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II, set to 20mm for a sweeping, natural-wide perspective. Camera settings: f/8 for edge-to-edge sharpness, ISO 100, 1/60s (tripod-mounted), circular polarizer + subtle 3-stop graduated ND to balance sky; remote release and focus on mid-ground. Time of day & light: golden hour / magic hour, ~20 minutes before sunset, sun low at ~15° above horizon, positioned camera-left rear so warm directional light skims across the villa and rice terraces from left-to-right. Light behavior: long, sculpting shadows across rice paddies; warm specular highlights on polished teak, soft rim-light on volcanic stone edges, glass reflecting sky with controlled highlights; hidden warm uplighting under eaves and soft amber LED pool rim lights just beginning to glow; gentle interior warm glow through window louvers. Sky: mostly clear with thin wispy cirrus clouds adding drama, not overcast. Composition: camera height 1.2 m (low eye level) placed on the edge of a wet volcanic stone pathway so the stones form a leading line into the frame toward the villa; frangipani branch and large palm leaf subtly frame upper right for depth; villa mass aligned on the right third of the frame, infinity-edge pool and terraced rice paddies occupying left third. Depth layers: foreground — wet volcanic stone steps, dew-specked rice blades and a teak daybed with a neatly folded white towel and a clay bowl (lived-in but minimal); mid-ground — open-plan teak pavilion, carved doors, textured volcanic stone walls and glass; background — layered rice terraces, distant palms and a hint of volcanic silhouette. Atmosphere & mood: warm golden highlights with cool blue shadows, slight golden haze for softness, crystal-clear air, smooth creamy bokeh on foreground frangipani petals and distant palms. Micro-details: crisp water reflections with subtle ripples, visible teak grain and joinery, veining and porous texture of volcanic stone, beads of water on rice leaves, glass clarity with natural reflections. Style: photorealistic, magazine-quality Architectural Digest/Wallpaper* cover-worthy, no people, aspirational yet lived-in luxury, include landscaping and tasteful outdoor furniture only as described.

name: swiss-chalet
property: Swiss alpine chalet with massive panoramic windows facing snow-capped mountains, warm timber and stone exterior
prompt: Sony A7R V with Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II at 20mm, tripod-mounted; aperture f/8 for edge-to-edge architectural sharpness, ISO 100, shutter 1/60s. Golden hour / magic hour daylight: sun low (≈10° above horizon) camera-left-rear (west), casting long warm directional light across the timber facade and stone base; warm rim light on rooflines and eaves, deep, cool blue shadows on north-facing timber. Light interactions: crisp reflections of the snow-capped peaks and sky in the massive panoramic windows, gentle specular highlights on metal fastenings, warm glow from interior lamps visible through glazing; subtle hidden warm uplighting at the stone plinth and soft LED under-eave lighting to reveal texture. Sky: clear alpine sky with wispy cirrus streaks for drama. Camera position and composition: low, slightly elevated chest height (~1.2 m) with a 12° upward tilt to emphasize grandeur and the sweeping mountain vista; main chalet placed on the right vertical third, panoramic glass centered slightly right of center; leading lines from a snow-dusted timber deck and stone pathway draw the eye toward the entrance and windows, layered depth with foreground snow-laden evergreens and a stone terrace, mid-ground chalet, background jagged peaks and sky. Foreground framing: out-of-focus fir branches at bottom-left for depth (smooth, creamy bokeh), a carefully placed Adirondack chair with a folded wool blanket and steaming enamel mug on a side table to suggest lived-in luxury (no people). Mood, color and micro-detail: warm golden highlights on timber and stone, cool blue shadow tones, slight golden haze in the valley for atmosphere; razor-sharp textures — grain of hand-hewn timber, veining in local stone, crystalline snow on railings, perfect mountain reflections in glass. Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality, aspirational but authentic, no people, includes tasteful landscaping and outdoor furniture to enhance composition.

name: maldives-overwater
property: Floating overwater bungalow in the Maldives, turquoise lagoon, thatched roof with modern interior, wooden deck
prompt: Hasselblad X2D 100C with XCD 21mm f/4 wide-angle, tripod-mounted; settings: f/8 for edge-to-edge architectural sharpness, ISO 100, 1/125s shutter. Golden hour / magic hour daylight with the sun low on the western horizon, warm directional light coming from camera-left-rear (45° behind the camera) casting long, soft shadows across the wooden deck and thatched roof; sunlight skimming the water surface to create sparkling specular highlights and crisp reflections of the bungalow. Hidden warm LED uplighting under the deck lip and subtle interior warm glow through sliding glass doors; soft underwater deck lights adding a faint turquoise underglow. Sky: clear with a few high, wispy cirrus clouds for drama, no overcast. Camera position: low-angle at ~0.9 m (3 ft), placed on the corner of the deck to emphasize grandeur and lead the eye; compose the bungalow on the right third (rule of thirds) with the sweeping deck planks as leading lines from bottom-left foreground into the mid-ground structure. Depth layers: textured wet wood foreground (visible grain and salt sheen), mid-ground overwater bungalow with thatch and glass, background turquoise lagoon and distant palm silhouette on the horizon. Composition details: slight left-side offset so the walkway and railings frame the scene, strong horizontal lines balanced by vertical posts, negative space of expansive sky to convey serenity. Mood and grading: rich warm golden highlights, cool blue-turquoise shadows, high-contrast but natural grading, slight golden haze for atmosphere. Micro-details: razor-sharp thatch texture, visible wood joinery, lifelike water reflections mirroring roof and sky, tiny droplets and wet sheen on planks, creamy bokeh on distant palms, crystal-clear glass reflections. Photorealistic, magazine-quality cover image (Architectural Digest/Wallpaper*), aspirational yet lived-in luxury, NO PEOPLE; include subtle outdoor furniture and a neatly folded towel only if it enhances composition.

name: hollywood-hills-modern
property: Modernist hillside house in the Hollywood Hills, cantilevered design, city lights panorama at dusk
prompt: Hasselblad X2D 100C with Hasselblad XCD 21mm f/4 wide-angle lens — architectural sweep; settings: f/8, ISO 100, shutter 1/4s on tripod, RAW capture, tethered remote release for absolute sharpness. Golden hour / magic hour, ~12–18 minutes before sunset, sun low (~15° above horizon) to the west-southwest, warm directional light grazing the cantilevered planes from the left, producing long dramatic shadows and rim-light on the roofline; glass catching warm sky reflections; subtle hidden linear LED uplighting under the cantilever and warm interior glow through windows; pool soft underwater lights just beginning to assert themselves; sky clear with wispy cirrus for texture. Camera position: low-angle grandeur, camera placed ~1.2 m (4 ft) above the terrace level, slightly tilted up to emphasize cantilever and city panorama; composition: main structure placed on the upper-right third (rule of thirds), leading lines from the pool edge, terrace, and minimalist driveway draw the eye toward a sweeping mid-ground of the cantilever and a background panorama of Hollywood Hills down to the city lights; foreground sculpted drought-tolerant landscaping (agave, textured pavers) for depth. Depth layers: foreground plants and stone, mid-ground architecture and terrace, background LA cityscape and distant hills. Mood and grading: warm golden highlights with cool deep-blue shadows, rich earth tones in wood and concrete, slight golden haze over the city for atmospheric depth; bokeh: smooth round highlights from distant city lights and pool reflections, creamy out-of-focus flora. Micro-details: tactile concrete texture, fine wood grain on cladding, razor-clear glass, mirror-like pool reflection of cantilever and sky. Style: photorealistic, magazine-cover quality (Architectural Digest / Wallpaper*), aspirational but lived-in luxury, include one modern teak lounge with a neatly draped throw, NO PEOPLE in frame, no CGI or render — must read as a real photograph that makes the viewer say “I need to live there.”

name: provence-farmhouse
property: Provence countryside stone farmhouse with lavender fields, terracotta roof, rustic luxury renovation
prompt:
Hasselblad X2D 100C with XCD 21mm f/4 (wide-angle); settings: f/8, ISO 100, 1/125s — tripod-stable, RAW capture. Golden hour / magic hour daylight: sun low (≈15° above horizon) positioned slightly behind camera-left (west-northwest) casting warm directional light across the lavender rows toward the farmhouse, long soft shadows and a warm rim on the terracotta roof tiles. Light interaction: warm sunlight grazing the weathered stone façade, bright highlights and soft reflections in the windows, subtle warm interior glow through mullions, delicate specular highlights on aged wood and iron hardware; hidden low uplights at the base of the façade and gentle ground lights between lavender rows to accentuate texture without looking artificial. Sky: clear with thin wispy cirrus for drama, slight golden haze near horizon.

Composition: camera placed about 25 meters from the house at 1.2 m height (slightly low chest level) for grandeur and intimacy; frame the farmhouse on the right vertical third with the main gable aligned to the upper-right intersection (rule of thirds). Leading lines: meticulously planted lavender rows starting in the lower-left foreground lead the eye into the mid-ground courtyard and front door. Foreground: rich lavender in bloom with a few soft-focus stalks close to lens for depth; mid-ground: rustic luxury renovated stone farmhouse with terracotta roof, shuttered windows, reclaimed wood doors, stone courtyard with simple outdoor dining vignette; background: rolling olive and cypress-studded hills and sky. Depth layers: foreground lavender, mid-ground architecture, background hills.

Mood & grading: warm golden highlights, cool blue-violet shadows in lavender, rich earth tones in stone and wood, filmic but hyperreal color fidelity. Atmosphere: crystal-clear air with a faint golden haze, tactile micro-details — grain of stone, veining in terracotta, dew on lavender tips, glass clarity with subtle reflections. Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality (Architectural Digest / Wallpaper*), aspirational yet lived-in luxury, no people, no CGI — a real photographed moment that stops the scroll.

name: amalfi-coast-villa
property: Coastal cliffside villa in Amalfi Coast, Mediterranean architecture, terraced gardens cascading toward the sea
prompt: Sony A7R V with Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II (wide-angle), shot at 24mm, aperture f/8, ISO 100, shutter 1/125s, tripod-mounted, RAW-quality capture. Golden-hour / magic-hour daylight: sun low (~15° above horizon) camera-left rear (warm light angling from left-back toward the sea) creating long, soft shadows across the terraced gardens and stairways; warm directional highlights grazing lemon-washed stucco, textured volcanic stone, and reflecting off glass balustrades and the infinity pool. Supplementary subtle lighting: hidden warm uplights under stair treads and terraces, soft cool-blue pool LEDs just beginning to glow, warm interior window glow for depth. Sky: clear with high, wispy cirrus clouds for drama, slight golden haze at horizon. Camera position and framing: low‑angle (camera height ~1.0 m) placed on an adjacent seaward terrace ~10 m from the main façade, slightly left-of-center so the villa sits on the right third of the frame, horizon on the upper third; use leading lines of terraced stone walls, descending steps and olive trees from lower-left into the main entrance to draw the eye. Depth layers: foreground bougainvillea and terracotta pots with creamy foreground bokeh, mid-ground villa with cascading terraces, infinity pool and teak loungers with a casually draped linen throw (lived-in luxury), background Tyrrhenian Sea and distant coastline. Mood & grading: warm golden highlights, cool blue shadows, rich earth and terracotta tones, slight salty haze for atmosphere; ultra-sharp micro-detail on stucco texture, grain of reclaimed wood shutters, veining in stone, razor-clear glass reflections and mirror-like pool surface. Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality (Architectural Digest/Wallpaper*), no people, aspirational yet believable coastal living.

name: japanese-zen-house
property: Japanese-inspired minimalist house with zen garden, Kyoto aesthetic, clean lines, natural materials, koi pond
prompt: Photorealistic, magazine-quality exterior photograph of a Japanese-inspired minimalist house with a Zen garden and koi pond — Kyoto aesthetic, clean lines, natural cedar and stone, lived-in luxury, no people. CAMERA & LENS: Sony A7R V; Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II set to 24mm. CAMERA SETTINGS: aperture f/8 for edge-to-edge sharpness, ISO 100, shutter 1/60s, tripod-mounted for maximum detail. LIGHTING (golden hour / magic hour daylight): sun low (~20° above horizon) positioned to the left-rear (west-southwest) creating warm directional light that skims cedar cladding and eaves, long soft shadows cast forward-right; warm rim light on rooflines, delicate highlights on shoji screens and metal fittings; subtle interior warm glow through sliding screens; supplementary hidden lighting — soft uplight on the stone lantern and under-water pond lights to reveal koi, and a thin warm LED strip under the engawa for depth; sky clear with wispy high clouds for drama. COMPOSITION: low, slightly elevated camera height ~1.2 m (chest height) on the approach path, 24mm wide-angle to capture layers; leading lines formed by stepping stones, manicured moss and bamboo fence draw the eye into the frame; main house placed on the right third of the frame with the koi pond and engawa in the mid-ground and maples/bamboo grove in the background; foreground textured stones and moss provide scale and depth; rule of thirds composition with depth layers: foreground moss and stone lantern, mid-ground pond and veranda, background trees and sky. ATMOSPHERE & MOOD: warm golden highlights and cool blue-violet shadows, slight golden haze in the air for softness, creamy bokeh on distant maple leaves, razor-sharp micro-details — cedar grain, fine veining in stepping stones, water ripples and reflections of eaves and koi, subtle shadow of tatami pattern through shoji; aspirational but real, minimal outdoor teak bench and bamboo water spout included to enhance composition. STYLE: hyper-real, not CGI, cover-quality for Architectural Digest / Wallpaper*, no people, calm, inviting, timeless.

name: joshua-tree-retreat
property: Desert modern retreat in Joshua Tree, rammed earth walls, expansive glass, desert landscape with boulders and cacti
prompt:
Sony A7R V with Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II, focal length 20mm, tripod-mounted; exposure f/8, ISO 100, shutter 1/125s for crisp handheld-level sharpness (or bracket for +/-1.5 EV if needed). Golden hour / magic hour daylight, sun low in the west ~18° above horizon, warm directional light coming from camera-right (west‑southwest) grazing the rammed earth walls and casting long, sculpting shadows across textured surfaces. Glass facades catch subtle sky reflections; interior warm glow visible through select panes; discreet hidden uplighting along the pathway and subtle amber pool lights just switching on to add depth.

Sky: clear with thin wispy cirrus clouds to add drama without blocking light. Camera position: low chest height ~1.2 m, slight low-angle look to amplify the retreat’s presence, composed with the main volume on the left third of the frame (rule of thirds), wide negative space to the right showing desert expanse. Leading lines: low rammed-earth retaining wall and stone flagstone path begin in the foreground lower-left, guiding the eye into the entry and along the glass wall. Foreground: large native boulders, agave and teddy-bear cholla softly blurred to create a shallow foreground layer; mid-ground: architecture with expansive glass and overhanging eaves; background: distant Joshua Tree ridgeline and open sky.

Framing devices: overhanging roofline and upright cacti on right edge frame the composition. Depth: distinct foreground (boulders, plants), mid-ground (house), background (desert/mountains). Color palette: warm golden highlights, rich earth tones of rammed earth, cool blue-purple shadows—subtle teal‑orange grade for magazine polish. Air: slight golden haze near horizon for atmosphere, crystal-clear clarity in glass reflections. Micro-details emphasized: grain and horizontal stratification of rammed earth, fine wood grain under eaves, veining in stone pavers, crisp glass reflections, faint ripple reflections in pool water. Bokeh: creamy out‑of‑focus highlights on foreground succulents and distant highlights.

Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality (Architectural Digest/Wallpaper*), aspirational but lived-in luxury; include minimal modern outdoor furniture (a folded linen throw on a lounger) and native landscaping; NO people; NOT CGI.

name: scandinavian-lakehouse
property: Scandinavian lakeside cabin with black timber cladding, floor-to-ceiling glass, birch forest and still lake
prompt: Hasselblad X2D 100C with XCD 21mm f/4 (wide-angle) — 21mm, tripod-mounted; aperture f/8 for maximum architectural sharpness, ISO 100, shutter 1/60s, RAW capture. Golden hour / magic hour: shooting ~20 minutes before sunset, sun 15° above horizon positioned slightly behind camera to the left so warm directional light grazes the black timber cladding from left-rear, creating long, soft shadows and warm highlights on texture; subtle warm interior glow through floor-to-ceiling glass and concealed warm LED uplighting under eaves and along the dock to suggest lived-in warmth. Sky: clear with a few wispy cirrus clouds for drama, crystal-clear air with a slight golden haze over the lake surface. Camera position and composition: low, heroic angle at ~1.0 m height, 12–15 m from the cabin, slight 25° angle to the right so the dock/shoreline acts as a leading line from lower-left foreground into the cabin placed on the right vertical third (rule of thirds); birch trunks frame left and right edges as natural framing devices; layered depth — foreground mossy rocks and birch trunks, mid-ground dock, deck and cabin, background still lake, birch forest and distant hills. Light interactions and micro-details: mirror-like reflections of the cabin and birch in the still lake, glass clarity showing soft interior textiles, warm light catching the grain of black-stained timber, delicate veining in stone, dew on moss; long, defined shadows from birch branches across the facade. Mood and grading: warm golden highlights, cool blue-violet shadows for contrast, rich earth tones in wood and stone, subtle filmic contrast, creamy round bokeh on out-of-focus birch leaves. Style rules: photorealistic, NOT a render or CGI — magazine-cover quality for Architectural Digest/Wallpaper*/Dwell; no people in frame; aspirational yet believable lived-in luxury — tasteful teak lounge with a tossed wool throw on the deck and a small kettle on the table to enhance the story.

name: miami-penthouse
property: Ultra-modern Miami penthouse rooftop terrace, downtown skyline view, sleek furniture, rooftop infinity pool
prompt: Ultra-modern Miami penthouse rooftop terrace with a sleek infinity pool overlooking the downtown skyline and Biscayne Bay, photographed like a top-tier architectural photographer for Architectural Digest. Camera body: Sony A7R V. Lens: Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II at 16mm (wide-angle for sweeping exterior). Camera settings: aperture f/8 for edge-to-edge sharpness, ISO 100, shutter speed 1/160s, tripod-mounted for absolute precision. Golden hour / magic hour: sun low on the western horizon, slightly left-rear (west-southwest), warm directional light casting long, dramatic shadows across travertine decking; sunlight grazing the pool surface creating sparkling highlights and warm reflections on glass and polished metal. Supplementary lighting: subtle warm LED uplights hidden in planters, soft pool-edge lights glowing, and a low interior warm glow leaking through sliding glass doors to suggest lived-in luxury. Sky: clear with delicate wispy cirrus clouds for drama, no overcast. Composition: camera positioned low and inviting at 0.9 m (roughly eye-height seated), slight upward tilt (~10°) to include skyline; frame so the infinity pool edge and glass balustrade act as leading lines drawing the eye toward the skyline. Place the skyline and sun on the upper third, pool and terrace occupying the lower two thirds (rule of thirds). Foreground: sculptural potted palms, a pair of minimalist loungers with a casually draped linen towel and a glass of rosé on a side table; mid-ground: seamless pool, integrated fire pit and sleek outdoor dining; background: luminous downtown towers and bay. Depth layers: strong foreground textures, mid-ground water reflections, distant city bokeh. Mood & grading: warm golden highlights with cool blue-violet shadows, slight golden atmospheric haze from coastal humidity, magazine-grade rich earth tones and crisp contrast. Micro-details: razor-sharp travertine grain, brushed stainless hardware, flawless glass clarity, tiny water ripples and tile gleam, creamy bokeh on out-of-focus palms and distant lights. Photorealistic, not a CGI or render — magazine-cover quality, aspirational but unmistakably real, no people in frame.

name: caribbean-plantation
property: Caribbean colonial plantation house restored as luxury estate, palm-lined driveway, coral stone, ocean backdrop
prompt:
Hasselblad X2D 100C with XCD 21mm f/4 (wide-angle); tripod-mounted, RAW. Camera settings: f/8 for edge-to-edge sharpness, ISO 100, 1/60s, mirrorless long-exposure technique, shoot bracketed for highlight/shadow recovery. Scene: restored Caribbean colonial plantation house luxury estate at golden hour / magic hour daylight. Sun low (~18° above horizon) positioned camera-left-rear, warm directional light grazing the coral stone facade at a ~30° incident angle, producing long, dramatic shadows along the palm‑lined driveway and creating rim-light on palm fronds. Light interactions: warm sunlight catching stone texture and whitewashed wood trim, specular highlights and clean reflections in large glass panes, subtle warm interior glow through plantation-shuttered windows, hidden warm uplighting softly washing columns and entry, soft blue pool/landscape lights beginning to glow for contrast. Sky: clear with thin wispy cirrus clouds and a slight golden haze at the ocean horizon.

Composition: low, grand chest-height viewpoint (≈1.2 m) to emphasize scale; house placed on the right third of the frame (rule of thirds) with the curving coral‑stone, palm‑lined driveway as a leading line sweeping from lower-left foreground into the mid-ground entrance. Foreground: textured coral pavers, manicured hedges and a low reflecting pool edge capturing facade and sky reflections; framing devices: an overhanging palm frond top-left and a low stone planter creating depth. Mid-ground: main colonial architecture—verandas, columns, shuttered windows, teak outdoor furniture subtly staged. Background: glittering ocean horizon and pastel sky beyond. Depth layers clearly separated: foreground texture, mid-ground architecture, background sea/sky.

Mood & grading: warm golden highlights, cool blue-violet shadows, rich earth tones in coral stone and teak; slight golden atmospheric haze toward horizon. Micro-details: visible coral stone grain, wood grain on shutters and teak furniture, crystal-clear glass reflections, smooth creamy bokeh on distant palms. Style rules: photorealistic, magazine-cover quality (Architectural Digest/Wallpaper*), NOT a render or CGI, NO people in the frame, aspirational but lived-in luxury, include landscaping/outdoor furniture only to enhance composition, correct verticals and naturalistic color grading.

name: tuscan-hilltop-villa
property: Tuscan hilltop villa with cypress tree-lined approach, honey-colored stone, vineyard views, Renaissance proportions
prompt:
Phase One XF IQ4 150MP + Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II (set to 24mm) — wide-angle architectural capture. Settings: f/8, ISO 100, 1/125s, tripod-mounted, RAW, tethered capture. Golden hour / magic hour daylight, sun low on the western horizon, warm directional light coming from left-rear at ~30° behind the villa, casting long, soft cypress shadows along the approach and creating rim light on roof tiles. Light interaction: warm sunlight kissing honey-colored stone masonry and terracotta roof, strong texture shadows in carved stone, soft reflections on leaded glass windows, subtle interior warm glow through shutters, and low concealed uplighting at the villa base and cypress trunks to retain texture in shadowed areas. Sky: clear with a few wispy high clouds to add drama without blocking sun.

Composition: camera positioned low and slightly left-of-center on the cypress-lined gravel drive, height ~1.2 m (4 ft) for grandeur; 24mm for sweeping approach that creates leading lines from cypress row and gravel ruts toward the main entrance. Place the villa on the upper left third of the frame (rule of thirds) with foreground lavender beds, a sunlit stone fountain and olive tree to add scale and lived-in detail; mid-ground features the honey-stone facade, loggia and terracotta roof; background shows rolling vineyard rows and distant hills. Use leading lines of the drive and cypresses to draw the eye; include depth layers and diagonals for dynamic perspective.

Atmosphere & mood: warm golden highlights, cool blue-violet shadows, slight golden haze over vineyards for depth, crystal-clear air. Color grading: rich earth tones, elevated warmth on stone, deep cool shadows for contrast. Bokeh: smooth creamy separation on distant vines and hills. Micro-details: visible grain and veining in stone, weathered wood shutters, iron balcony patina, reflective pool ripple gently mirroring sky, dew on lavender, crisp shadow fringing on cobbles.

Photorealistic magazine-quality photograph (not CGI), aspirational yet lived-in, no people, include tasteful outdoor furniture or a classic Fiat 500 only if it enhances composition.

name: big-sur-mobile-home
property: Luxury Airstream-style modern mobile home in Big Sur, polished aluminum, redwood forest setting, elevated deck platform
prompt: Sony A7R V with Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II (set to 24mm), tripod-mounted, aperture f/8, ISO 100, shutter 1/125s, RAW — golden hour, warm directional sunlight 20° above the western horizon coming from camera-left, casting long, soft side-light and elongated shadows across the elevated deck and polished aluminum Airstream-style exterior; sun creates crisp specular highlights and warm streaked reflections on the curved aluminum skin while rich amber light grazes the redwood siding and deck. Camera position low-angle, 1.1 m (3.5 ft) height, slight upward tilt to emphasize the elevated platform and the Airstream’s silhouette, composition framed with a foreground sweep of native coastal scrub, a leading line of the redwood boardwalk/deck rail drawing the eye into the mid-ground where the trailer sits slightly right of center (rule of thirds), background of towering redwoods with a distant glint of Pacific through the trunks and a clear sky with wispy cirrus clouds for drama. Include layered depth: mossy roots and ferns in soft foreground, teak lounge chairs and a folded wool throw on the deck as subtle lived-in details, elevated deck with hidden warm uplighting beneath its lip and a gentle interior warm glow through frosted windows to create inviting contrast. Emphasize micro-texture: visible grain and sun-warmed patina of redwood, crisp peening and rivet details on polished aluminum, glass clarity with restrained reflections, creamy out-of-focus redwood bokeh in the background. Lighting balance: primary golden-hour sun as key, subtle bounce/refill from the right with a soft silver reflector to preserve shadow detail, no harsh artificial fills. Color palette: warm golden highlights, cool blue-violet shadows filtering through the forest, rich red-brown wood tones — subtle filmic contrast and magazine-grade color grading (warm highlights, cool shadows, high micro-contrast). Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality — top-tier architectural photograph, no people, aspirational but clearly real and lived-in, emphasize scale, texture, and the seductive promise of Big Sur living.

name: cape-town-clifftop
property: Cape Town clifftop residence with Atlantic Ocean panorama, contemporary African design, fynbos landscaping, Table Mountain backdrop
prompt:
Sony A7R V with Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II, wide-angle set to 20mm, handheld or tripod-mounted for absolute sharpness. Exposure: f/8 for architectural depth, ISO 100, shutter 1/125s. Golden hour / magic hour daylight: sun low over the Atlantic (west–southwest), positioned slightly left-rear of camera at ~15° elevation so warm directional light grazes the facade and terraces, long dramatic shadows stretch toward the camera. Light interaction: warm rim and side light on natural stone cladding and timber eaves, brilliant reflections on full-height glazing, delicate specular highlights on polished metal balustrades; hidden warm LED uplighting beneath cantilevered eaves and subtle cyan pool LEDs create layered glow; interior windows show a soft warm interior light. Sky: clear with long wispy cirrus for drama, no overcast.

Camera position and composition: low-angle view at approximately 1.0 m height, camera placed slightly left-of-center about 6–8 m from the terrace to include sweeping infinity pool and Atlantic panorama; use pool edge, teak decking seams and a fynbos planting path as leading lines that pull the eye to the ocean and Table Mountain. Rule of thirds: main building mass sits on the right third, infinity pool and ocean occupy the lower and middle thirds, Table Mountain silhouette frames upper-left third. Depth layers: foreground sculptural fynbos and native proteas (soft bokeh), mid-ground teak terrace, pool and modern low-profile outdoor sofa with sculptural firepit, background Atlantic Ocean and Table Mountain.

Mood and color grading: warm golden highlights with cool blue-violet shadows, rich earth tones in stone and wood, slight golden haze over the ocean for atmosphere, crystal-clear air. Micro-details: razor-sharp wood grain on decking, veining in local stone, flawless glass clarity with subtle double-reflection, mirror-like pool surface reflecting sky and mountain, salt spray glimmer on cliff edge. Photorealistic, magazine-quality — cover-worthy Architectural Digest / Wallpaper* aesthetic; no people, aspirational yet lived-in, include only tasteful landscaping and minimal outdoor furniture to enhance composition, no CGI or render.

name: mykonos-cycladic
property: Greek island cycladic house on Mykonos, whitewashed walls, cobalt blue accents, Aegean Sea view, bougainvillea
prompt: Hasselblad X2D 100C with XCD 21mm f/4 (wide-angle) — settings: f/8, ISO 100, 1/125s on tripod. Golden hour / magic hour daylight, sun low in the west (~20° above horizon) casting warm directional light from camera-left-rear; light grazes and sculpts the whitewashed cycladic volumes, throws long soft shadows across curved steps and terraces, creates warm highlights on cobalt-blue doors, shutters and railings, and produces subtle specular reflections on any glass and the distant sea. Supplementary lighting: very subtle warm LED uplighting hidden beneath terrace lips and steps, and a soft warm interior glow through a window to suggest lived-in luxury. Sky: clear with thin wispy cirrus for drama, no heavy clouds. Camera position: elevated 1.8 m (on a small terrace or step), approximately 6 m from the main façade, slight 5° downward tilt to include forecourt and terrace; low-to-mid wide perspective that reads grand yet intimate. Composition: house mass placed on the left third, horizon on the upper third; leading lines from whitewashed steps, curved parapet and a narrow stone path guide the eye toward the Aegean; foreground bougainvillea (lower-left) provides framing and soft pop of magenta, mid-ground architecture shows cubic forms and cobalt accents, background is the glittering Aegean and sky for depth. Mood & color grading: warm golden highlights with cool blue shadows and rich earth accents, slight golden haze for atmosphere, creamy smooth bokeh on out-of-focus bougainvillea, razor-sharp micro-detail — limewash texture, veining in stone pavers, weathered wood grain of shutters, crisp paint edges, shimmering sea reflections. Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality (Architectural Digest/Wallpaper*-level), no people, aspirational yet lived-in luxury; tasteful minimal outdoor furniture (white linen lounger) and abundant bougainvillea only as compositional accents.

name: norwegian-fjord-glass
property: Norwegian fjord glass house, dramatic cliff setting, mirrored facade reflecting water, aurora-ready design
prompt:
Sony A7R V with Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II at 24mm, tripod-mounted; aperture f/8 for edge-to-edge sharpness, ISO 100, shutter 1/60s. Golden hour / magic hour daylight, sun low on the western horizon (behind and slightly left of camera) casting warm, directional light across the fjord and skimming the glass facade at a 25–30° angle; long, soft shadows falling diagonally across the cliff face and decking. Clear sky with wispy cirrus clouds for drama.

Camera position and composition: low-angle, 1.2 m (4 ft) camera height, 5.5 m (18 ft) back from the glass edge, slightly off-center to the right so the cantilevered glass house sits on the right third of the frame; leading lines created by the rocky shoreline, a narrow wooden deck and a steel railing converge toward the entrance. Foreground: mossy rocks and low arctic pines left, soft-focus coastal grasses bottom-left (creamy bokeh), mid-ground: mirrored glass house cantilevering over the fjord, background: calm fjord water and distant snow-capped peaks. Depth layered: textured rocks → reflective water → architecture → mountain sky.

Lighting interactions: mirrored facade returns crisp reflections of water and sky, specular highlights on glass edges, warm rim light on exposed timber and corten steel, hidden warm uplighting beneath the cantilever and recessed LED glow from a few interior rooms to suggest lived-in luxury without people. Sky light produces cool blue shadows that contrast with golden highlights; slight golden haze above horizon for atmosphere.

Micro-details: razor-sharp rendering of wood grain, veining in natural stone, tiny water ripples and wet rock sheen, clear mirror reflections with faint smearing from sea spray, subtle condensation on metal fasteners. Color grading: warm golden highlights, deep cool-blue shadows, rich earth tones in materials — photorealistic, magazine-quality, cover-ready for Architectural Digest or Wallpaper*. No people; include one sculptural lounge chair on deck and minimal landscape planting to enhance scale and aspiration. Not CGI — must read like a real photograph.

name: tulum-treehouse
property: Tulum jungle treehouse villa, sustainable bamboo and concrete, cenote pool, dense tropical canopy
prompt:
Sony A7R V, Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II at 16mm — tripod-mounted; aperture f/8, ISO 100, shutter 1/30s, RAW, polarizer to manage reflections. Golden hour (magic hour) daylight: sun low behind the left jungle canopy (~12° above horizon), warm directional light coming from camera-left rear at ~30° relative to the camera axis, creating long soft shadows and rim-light on bamboo and foliage. Light interaction: warm light grazing the bamboo poles and textured concrete, crisp golden highlights on rattan furniture, specular reflections and perfect mirror-like shimmer with slight ripple on the cenote pool; interior windows emit a soft warm glow; subtle hidden warm uplighting under the elevated deck and soft turquoise pool lights for layered luminosity. Sky: clear with wispy cirrus clouds for drama.

Composition: low-angle perspective, camera height 1.1 m (3.6 ft) for grandeur, villa placed on the left third (rule of thirds), leading lines from a reclaimed-wood boardwalk and braided rope railing guiding the eye into the mid-ground; foreground lush palm fronds and ferns partially frame top-right and left for intimate depth; mid-ground elevated bamboo-and-concrete treehouse with cantilevered deck and woven hammock; lower-right third shows the cenote pool reflecting the sky and architecture; background dense tropical canopy and distant pale sky. Strong depth layers: wet stone edging and textured concrete in immediate foreground, mid-ground architecture, background jungle.

Atmosphere & mood: warm golden tones on highlights, cool blue-green shadows in the understory, rich earth tones in materials; slight golden haze in the air for a dreamy, lived-in resort feel; creamy smooth bokeh on out-of-focus leaves and distant canopy. Micro-details emphasized: hand-woven joinery in bamboo, grain and veining in cast concrete, water droplets on leaves, crisp glass clarity with minimal reflection. Photorealistic, magazine-cover quality, no people, aspirational yet believable — include minimal styled outdoor furniture (one rattan lounger, hammock) to suggest life without clutter.

name: lake-como-palazzo
property: Lake Como Italian palazzo with private dock, ornate balustrades, manicured gardens, Alps reflected in the lake
prompt: Sony A7R V + Sony FE 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II at 24mm, tripod-mounted, f/8, ISO 100, 1/125s — ultra-sharp magazine-grade architectural capture. Golden hour / magic hour daylight, sun low in the west just off-camera left, warm directional light grazing the ornate balustrades and stone façade, long soft shadows cast toward the lake; subtle rim highlight on glass windows and warm interior glow visible through a few lit rooms. Hidden warm uplighting accenting column bases and balustrade details, soft warm LED dock lights along the private pier. Sky: clear with a few high wispy cirrus clouds for drama, calm lake surface mirroring the Alps with crisp reflections and gentle golden shimmer. Camera position: low-angle grandeur at 1.0 m (3.3 ft) height, placed on the right-hand edge of a manicured stone path so the path and private dock form leading lines from the left foreground into the mid-ground palazzo; main villa aligned on the right third of the frame (rule of thirds) with layered depth — foreground manicured gardens and clipped box hedges, mid-ground terrace, ornate balustrades and private dock with a classic wooden boat (no people), background Lake Como and Alps reflected. Composition uses natural framing from trimmed cypress and a sculpted topiary, generous negative space in the luminous sky to breathe. Mood and color grading: warm golden highlights, cool blue-purple shadows, rich earth tones in stone and wood, slight golden haze over the distant mountains for depth. Photorealistic, hyper-detailed: visible stone veining, wrought-iron texture, wood grain on dock boards, glass clarity with subtle reflection artifacts, creamy bokeh on distant reeds and garden flowers. Magazine-cover quality, aspirational but lived-in luxury, no people, realistic lighting and materials — a breathtaking, stop-scrolling image.
